metadata.title: ICH Regulatory Rules Extraction
metadata.description: Complete extraction of all actionable pharmaceutical regulatory rules from ICH M4S(R2), Q5D, and Q3C(R9) guidelines

M4S_R2.title: The Common Technical Document for the Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use: Safety — M4S(R2)
M4S_R2.scope: Module 2 Nonclinical Summaries (2.4 and 2.6) and Module 4 Nonclinical Study Reports in the CTD

module_2_4_nonclinical_overview.title: Nonclinical Overview
module_2_4_nonclinical_overview.description: Integrated critical assessment of the pharmacological and toxicological evaluation of the drug

rules.rule: The Nonclinical Overview must be an integrated critical assessment, not a series of individual study summaries
Maximum length for Nonclinical Overview is approximately 30 pages
The Nonclinical Overview must be written by a qualified expert and reflect integrated interpretation, not mere data listing
The Nonclinical Overview must address the relevance of the animal findings to human safety
The Nonclinical Overview must explicitly discuss deficiencies in the nonclinical program and their impact on risk assessment

required_sections_sequence.title: Overview of the Nonclinical Testing Strategy

rules.rule: Describe the overall strategy for nonclinical testing including why particular studies were or were not conducted
Address the GLP compliance status of all studies; non-GLP studies must be justified
Describe the test article: physical-chemical properties, impurity profile, and comparability to clinical material
Summarize the primary pharmacodynamic findings and their relevance to proposed clinical use
Address secondary pharmacodynamic findings including off-target receptor binding
Address safety pharmacology findings (CNS, cardiovascular, respiratory) and their clinical relevance
Address pharmacodynamic drug interactions if studied
Provide integrated assessment of absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME) across species
Compare nonclinical PK parameters to human PK data where available
Discuss the relevance of metabolite profiles between animal species and humans
Discuss plasma protein binding and its relevance to toxicological findings
Provide integrated assessment of all toxicity findings across all study types
Identify target organs of toxicity and dose/exposure relationships (NOAEL, LOAEL) for each
Discuss reversibility of observed toxicities
Calculate and discuss human safety margins based on AUC or other appropriate exposure metric comparisons
Discuss genotoxicity findings and their weight-of-evidence interpretation
Address carcinogenicity data and mode-of-action analysis for any tumors observed
Reproductive and developmental toxicity findings must be assessed for relevance to clinical use populations (pregnant women, women of childbearing potential)
Local tolerance findings must be assessed for the proposed clinical route(s) of administration
Provide integrated benefit-risk conclusions from nonclinical perspective
Identify nonclinical issues relevant to safe use in humans and any monitoring requirements they suggest
Discuss consistency between nonclinical and clinical findings where clinical data are available

general_rules.rule: Section 2.6 consists of both Written Summaries and Tabulated Summaries; both are required
Written Summaries are factual summaries of the data; they are distinct from the interpretive Nonclinical Overview in 2.4
Tabulated Summaries use the standardized templates defined in Appendix B of M4S(R2)
Each study must be identified with its Location in CTD (Volume and Section number)
GLP compliance status must be indicated for each study in the tabulated summaries
For repeat-dose toxicity tabulated summaries (2.6.7.6), the highest NOAEL is underlined in the Doses column
Studies in tabulated summaries must be listed in the same order as they appear in the CTD
general_rules.source: M4S(R2) Appendix B Table 2.6.7.1 note 2, Table 2.6.7.2 note 2

general_rules.rule: Human data must be included for comparison in pharmacokinetic tabulated summaries where available

rules.rule: Brief introduction to the pharmacological class and intended clinical use; sets context for subsequent summaries
Written Summary must cover primary pharmacodynamics, secondary pharmacodynamics, safety pharmacology, and PD drug interactions in that sequence
In vitro studies must be presented before in vivo studies within each subsection
Tabulated summaries must cover primary pharmacology, secondary pharmacology, safety pharmacology, and PD drug interactions
PK Written Summary must cover: analytical methods, absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, drug interactions, and other studies in that sequence
Human PK data should be included for comparison where available

templates.title: Pharmacokinetics: Overview (list of all PK studies)
Pharmacokinetics: Analytical Methods and Validation Reports
Pharmacokinetics: Distribution — Placental Transfer and Secretion into Milk
Pharmacokinetics: Metabolism In Vitro
templates.note: Human data should be included for comparison if available

templates.title: Pharmacokinetics: Possible Metabolic Pathways
templates.note: Illustrate possible metabolic map indicating species in which metabolic reactions occur

templates.title: Pharmacokinetics: Excretion
templates.note: One column per study; human data at maximum recommended dose for comparison

templates.title: Pharmacokinetics: Excretion into Bile
templates.note: Data may be tabulated as in format of 2.6.5.13

rules.rule: Each PK tabulated summary template requires: Test Article (INN), Location in CTD (Vol. Section), Study Number
Excretion table (2.6.5.13): other routes such as biliary and respiratory should be added if performed
Toxicokinetics overview (2.6.7.3): a 1- to 3-page summary of steady-state TK data in a format facilitating cross-species comparisons including humans
Toxicology Written Summary must cover all seven subsections in sequence: single-dose, repeat-dose, genotoxicity, carcinogenicity, reproductive/developmental, local tolerance, other
Within repeat-dose toxicity, studies are ordered: rodent before non-rodent; within same species, shorter duration before longer duration
Within each species, routes of administration are ordered: clinical route first, then other routes

templates.title: Toxicology: Overview of All Toxicology Studies

rules.rule: One line per toxicology report in the same order as the CTD
For Repeat-Dose Toxicity, the highest NOAEL (No Observed Adverse-Effect Level) is underlined in the Doses column
rules.source: M4S(R2) Appendix B Table 2.6.7.1 footnote a

rules.rule: The location of each Technical Report in the CTD must be indicated
One line per toxicokinetics report in the same order as the CTD (Section 3, Toxicology)
A 1- to 3-page summary (tables and/or figures) of steady-state toxicokinetic data prepared in a format that facilitates comparisons across species including humans
All batches used in toxicology studies must be listed in approximate chronological order
Toxicology studies in which each batch was used must be identified
All single-dose toxicity studies must be summarized in the same order as the CTD; footnotes should indicate special features such as unusual duration, infusion rate, or age of test subjects
All repeat-dose toxicity studies including range-finding studies, OTHER than the definitive GLP studies specified by ICH Guideline M3, must be summarized here in the same order as the CTD

templates.title: Repeat-Dose Toxicity: Pivotal Studies (one table per study, numbered 2.6.7.7A, 2.6.7.7B, etc.)
body_fields: Noteworthy Findings including: Died or Sacrificed Moribund, Body Weight %, Food Consumption %, Water Consumption, Clinical Observations, Ophthalmoscopy, Electrocardiography, Hematology, Serum Chemistry, Urinalysis, Organ Weights %, Gross Pathology, Histopathology, Additional Examinations, Postdose Evaluation

rules.rule: One table per pivotal repeat-dose toxicity study; tables numbered consecutively 2.6.7.7A, 2.6.7.7B, 2.6.7.7C etc.
One table per each repeat-dose toxicity study specified by ICH Guideline M3 AND any other repeat-dose study that could be considered pivotal
ONLY NOTEWORTHY FINDINGS should be presented; additional parameters showing noteworthy changes should be added to the table
Postdose Evaluation section must list all parameters still showing drug-related changes; delete this section if study does not include a postdose evaluation period
Body weight and food consumption shown as percent difference from controls; group means shown for controls
rules.source: M4S(R2) Appendix B Table 2.6.7.7 footnote a

rules.rule: Statistical significance indicated: * = p<0.05, ** = p<0.01
Severity scale: - (no noteworthy findings), + (mild), ++ (moderate), +++ (marked)

templates.title: Genotoxicity: In Vitro Studies (numbered 2.6.7.8A, 2.6.7.8B, etc.)
header_fields: Vehicles (for Test Article and for Positive Controls)
templates.body: Results table with Metabolic Activation (Without/With) vs Test Article and Concentration/Dose Level

rules.rule: Results of replicate assays must be shown on subsequent pages; tables numbered consecutively 2.6.7.8A, 2.6.7.8B etc.
If precipitation is observed, this must be inserted in a footnote

templates.title: Carcinogenicity (numbered 2.6.7.10A, 2.6.7.10B, etc.; one per study)
body_fields: Number of Animals (At Start, Died/Sacrificed Moribund, Terminal Sacrifice)
Number of Animals with Neoplastic Lesions
Noteworthy Findings (Gross Pathology, Histopathology - Non-Neoplastic Lesions)

rules.rule: Body weight shown at 6 months; percent differences from controls for treated groups; statistical significance based on actual data
rules.source: M4S(R2) Appendix B Table 2.6.7.10 footnote a

general_rules.rule: Module 4 contains the full technical reports of all nonclinical studies
Reports within each category are ordered: rodent species before non-rodent species
Within same species, shorter duration studies before longer duration studies
Within same duration, in vitro studies before in vivo studies
Route of administration ordering within same species/duration: clinical route first, then other routes
Literature references may be included in relevant sections if they provide data relevant to the drug

4.1.title: Table of Contents of Module 4
4.1.rule: Must be provided listing all nonclinical reports

subsections.4.2.2.1: Analytical Methods and Validation Reports (if separate)

4.2.3.1.title: Single-Dose Toxicity
4.2.3.1.ordering_rule: Ordered by species (rodent first), then by route of administration (clinical route first)

4.2.3.2.title: Repeat-Dose Toxicity
4.2.3.2.ordering_rule: Ordered by species (rodent first), then by duration (shortest first), then by route (clinical route first)

4.2.3.3.title: Genotoxicity
4.2.3.3.ordering_rule: In vitro before in vivo within each subsection

subsections.4.2.3.5.3: Prenatal and postnatal development, including maternal function
subsections.4.2.3.5.4: Studies in which offspring (juvenile animals) are dosed and/or further evaluated

subsections.4.2.3.7: Other Toxicity Studies (antigenicity, immunotoxicity, mechanistic studies, dependence, metabolites, impurities, other)

placement_rules.rule: Pharmacology studies (primary PD, secondary PD, safety pharmacology, PD drug interactions) go in section 4.2.1
All ADME studies go in section 4.2.2 under the appropriate subsection (4.2.2.2 absorption, 4.2.2.3 distribution, 4.2.2.4 metabolism, 4.2.2.5 excretion)
Single-dose (acute) toxicity studies go in section 4.2.3.1
All repeat-dose toxicity studies (subacute, subchronic, chronic) go in section 4.2.3.2, ordered by species then duration then route
Genotoxicity in vitro studies go in 4.2.3.3.1; in vivo genotoxicity studies go in 4.2.3.3.2
Long-term carcinogenicity bioassays go in 4.2.3.4.1; short/medium-term carcinogenicity studies go in 4.2.3.4.2
Fertility and early embryonic development studies (ICH S5 Segment I) go in 4.2.3.5.1
Embryo-fetal development studies (ICH S5 Segment II, teratology) go in 4.2.3.5.2
Pre- and postnatal development studies (ICH S5 Segment III, peri- and postnatal) go in 4.2.3.5.3
Juvenile animal studies and studies where offspring are further evaluated go in 4.2.3.5.4
Studies on antigenicity, immunotoxicity, mechanistic studies, dependence potential, metabolites, impurities go in 4.2.3.7

Q5D.title: Derivation and Characterisation of Cell Substrates Used for Production of Biotechnological/Biological Products — ICH Q5D
Q5D.scope: Cell substrates used in production of biotechnological and biological products; covers source, history, generation, banking, characterization, and testing

rules.rule: A two-tier cell banking system is required: Master Cell Bank (MCB) followed by Working Cell Bank (WCB)
The MCB is a homogeneous suspension of cells distributed into individual containers in a single operation; it is derived from the original research cell bank or directly from the original cells
The WCB is prepared by expanding one or more containers of the MCB; it must be homogeneous and distributed into individual containers
All containers from a given cell bank (MCB or WCB) must be stored under identical conditions to ensure homogeneity
Documentation of the complete history (source, passage history, culture conditions, preparation procedures) must be maintained for both MCB and WCB
End-of-Production Cells (EOPC) — cells at or beyond the maximum population doubling level used in production — must also be characterized
The passage level (number of doublings or passages) at which the MCB was established must be defined and documented

tier_hierarchy.name: Master Cell Bank (MCB)
tier_hierarchy.description: Aliquots from a single homogeneous preparation stored in a single operation; forms the primary reference stock

tier_hierarchy.name: Working Cell Bank (WCB)
tier_hierarchy.description: Expanded from one or more MCB vials; used as the direct starting material for production
tier_hierarchy.testing_level: Subset of MCB testing required; must confirm identity and freedom from adventitious agents

tier_hierarchy.name: End-of-Production Cells (EOPC)
tier_hierarchy.description: Cells at or beyond the maximum in vitro cell age used in production
tier_hierarchy.testing_level: Tested to define the maximum production limit and confirm continued absence of adventitious agents

rules.rule: Identity of the cell substrate must be confirmed using at least one of: isoenzyme analysis, cytogenetic analysis, or DNA fingerprinting/short tandem repeat (STR) profiling
Isoenzyme analysis must be performed to confirm species of origin and detect interspecies cross-contamination
Cytogenetic analysis (karyology) should be performed for cells of human or primate origin; useful for confirming identity and detecting gross chromosomal abnormalities
DNA markers / DNA fingerprinting should be used where appropriate; species-specific probes or PCR-based methods are acceptable
Identity testing must be performed on the MCB; identity confirmation on the WCB is required
Bioburden testing must be performed on at least 1% of containers (minimum of 2 containers) from each cell bank
Testing for mycoplasma contamination must be performed on both the MCB and the WCB
Virus testing must be performed on the MCB and WCB; the scope of virus testing depends on the origin of the cell line and the manufacturing process
For human-derived cell lines, testing must include retroviruses and other relevant endogenous or adventitious viruses
For rodent-derived cell lines (e.g., CHO, NS0, Sp2/0), testing for endogenous retrovirus-like particles must be included
Tests for sterility (absence of bacteria and fungi) must be performed on the cell banks
The stability (viability and productivity characteristics) of cell banks must be assessed at a minimum of two time points during storage
Stability testing should include assessment of viability after thawing (post-thaw viability) and growth characteristics
If the cell bank is to be used for an extended period, periodic stability monitoring at defined intervals is required
The maximum number of population doublings from MCB to EOPC must be defined and validated to ensure genetic stability
Karyological analysis is required for new continuous cell lines of human or primate origin and for new diploid cell lines
Tumorigenicity testing in nude mice or other appropriate in vivo systems is required for continuous cell lines of human or primate origin
Karyological analysis must be performed on the MCB; if significant passage-dependent changes are expected, EOPC should also be evaluated
For well-established cell lines with extensive published characterization (e.g., CHO, BHK), tumorigenicity testing may be waived with justification

testing_matrix.description: Summary of required tests by cell bank tier

tests.MCB: Required for rodent and human lines
Required for human/primate and new diploid lines

rules.rule: For primary cells (non-established cell lines), a two-tier banking system is not always feasible; the documentation requirements still apply to the cell collection and preparation process
For primary cells, the source (donor history, age, health status), collection procedure, and any in vitro manipulation must be fully documented
Testing requirements for primary cells are similar to those for cell banks: identity, microbial sterility, mycoplasma, and virus testing

Q3C_R9.title: Impurities: Guideline for Residual Solvents — ICH Q3C(R9)
Q3C_R9.r9_change: Addition in section 3.4 of consideration of solvent volatility when selecting and validating analytical methods for Class 1 and Class 2 solvents
Q3C_R9.scope: Residual solvents in pharmaceutical products; establishes limits for solvents that should be restricted in drug substances, excipients, and drug products

rules.rule: Residual solvents must be reported when present above reporting thresholds; they must be identified and qualified
The total daily exposure to each solvent from all sources (drug substance, excipients, dosage form) must be considered; the sum must not exceed the PDE
If a manufacturer can demonstrate that a residual solvent has been reduced to a practical minimum and is below the established limit, no further testing or justification is needed
Use of Class 1 solvents should be avoided; if use cannot be avoided, levels must be restricted as defined in Table 1
Class 2 solvents should be limited in pharmaceutical products due to their inherent toxicity; limits are established in Table 2 via Option 1 or Option 2
Class 3 solvents have low toxic potential and do not need concentration limits if daily dose ≤50 mg/day; amounts >50 mg/day must be justified
Solvents in Table 4 (no toxicological data available) should be used with caution; manufacturers should supply justification for their use
Option 1: Use the concentration limits in Table 2 (ppm) directly in the drug substance or excipient without needing to know the daily dose

Option 2: Calculate the permitted concentration based on the actual daily dose of the drug product using the formula: Concentration (ppm) = 1000 × PDE / dose (g/day); this may allow higher concentrations in the ingredient if the daily dose is low
Option 2 requires knowledge of the maximum daily dose of the drug product; a default product mass of 10 g/day is used when dose is not known

pde_calculation_methodology.formula: PDE = NOEL × Weight Adjustment / (F1 × F2 × F3 × F4 × F5)
pde_calculation_methodology.concentration_formula: Concentration (ppm) = 1000 × PDE (mg/day) / dose (g/day) [assuming 10 g/day default product mass]

F2.description: Intraspecies variability factor for differences between individual humans

F3.note: F3=1 if NOEL from long-duration (>3 months in rodents or >1 month in non-rodents) study

rules.rule: PDE is calculated as: NOEL × 50 kg / (F1 × F2 × F3 × F4 × F5)
The NOEL used in PDE calculation must be the most appropriate (lowest relevant NOEL) from the most relevant and well-documented study
Concentration limit in ppm = (PDE in mg/day × 1000) / dose in g/day; default dose assumption is 10 g/day
Analytical methods must be validated for the solvents being tested; validation should include specificity, linearity, accuracy, and precision

R9 change: The volatility of Class 1 and Class 2 solvents should be considered when selecting and validating analytical methods; highly volatile solvents may require special handling to prevent losses during sample preparation
Headspace gas chromatography (HS-GC) is the preferred method for residual solvent analysis; other validated methods are acceptable
Methods must be capable of detecting solvents at or below the applicable limit (Class 1, Class 2, or Class 3 threshold)

class_1_solvents.description: Solvents to be avoided — known or suspected human carcinogens, or environmental hazards
class_1_solvents.rule: Use of Class 1 solvents should be avoided in the manufacture of drug substances, excipients, and drug products; if unavoidable, levels must not exceed Table 1 limits

class_2_solvents.description: Solvents to be limited — non-genotoxic animal carcinogens or possible causative agents of other irreversible toxicity; may cause other significant but reversible toxicities
class_2_solvents.rule: Class 2 solvent concentrations in drug products must not exceed the limits in Table 2 (ppm) based on Option 1, or the calculated limit based on Option 2 using the actual daily dose

part_calculations.note: F5 = 2.8 applied as intermediate value between 1 and 5; THF is a Class 2 solvent
NMP classified as Class 2; reproductive toxicant

part_calculations.calculation: PDE = 15 × 50 / (5 × 10 × 5 × 1 × 1) = 1.5 mg/day; final PDE 0.7 mg/day based on additional assessment
part_calculations.note: Cumene is a Class 2 solvent; hepatotoxicity and carcinogenicity concerns
TEA PDE exceeds 50 mg/day threshold; classified as Class 3 (low toxic potential)
PDE of 50 mg/day meets Class 3 threshold (≤50 mg/day); 2-MTHF placed in Table 3 (Class 3)
CPME placed in Table 2 (Class 2); F3=10 because duration <3 months

part_calculations.study_type: 2-year carcinogenicity (nephropathy in females; NOEL not established)
part_calculations.note: TBA placed in Table 2 (Class 2); F5=5 because NOEL was not established (LOEL used); ultimate PDE based on Scenario 1 (rat nephropathy LOEL=175 mg/kg/day)

class_3_solvents.description: Solvents with low toxic potential — GMP-limited; no health-based exposure limit needed if daily amount ≤50 mg/day
class_3_solvents.rule: Class 3 solvents may be present at levels up to 50 mg/day (or 5000 ppm in a 10 g/day dose) without justification; amounts above 50 mg/day require justification

class_4_no_toxicological_data.description: Solvents for which no adequate toxicological data were found; use with caution and justification required

rules.rule: Residual solvents must be tested if they are known or reasonably expected to be present in the drug substance, excipient, or product based on the manufacturing process
If a solvent is not used or produced in manufacturing but could theoretically be present from the environment, a risk-based approach may be used to decide whether testing is needed
